plainMeaning: On Mount Daśārṇakūṭa, to break the proud king Daśārṇabhadra's pride, Indra, lord of the gods, shows off his own dazzling celestial splendour.
recitation:
sele dasaṇṇakūḍe, dasaṇṇa-bhaddassa gavva-haraṇaṭṭhā |
sakko devāhivaï, niya-iḍḍhiṁ daṁsae evaṁ ||

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sela < Sanskrit śaila ('rock, mountain'); locative singular sele = 'on the mountain'.
wordByWord.notes: Sets the scene: the peak named in the next word.

wordByWord.etymology: Compound dasaṇṇa (< Daśārṇa, the country/region) + kūḍa (< Sanskrit kūṭa, 'summit, peak'); locative singular.
wordByWord.notes: The tīrtha this verse-cluster (vv.49–55) honours; the hill near Daśārṇapura where Mahāvīra's samavasaraṇa stood.

wordByWord.etymology: < Sanskrit Daśārṇa, the kingdom; here the first member of the royal name Daśārṇa-bhadra.
wordByWord.notes: Read together with the following word as the king's name.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bhadda < Sanskrit bhadra ('auspicious, noble'); genitive singular bhaddassa = 'of Bhadra'.
wordByWord.notes: Genitive governing gavva — 'the pride of Daśārṇabhadra'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit gavva < Sanskrit garva ('pride, conceit'); first member of the compound with haraṇaṭṭhā.
wordByWord.notes: The king's vanity that he could worship the Jina more grandly than anyone in the three worlds.

wordByWord.etymology: haraṇa (< Sanskrit haraṇa, 'taking away, removal') + aṭṭha (< artha, 'purpose'), dative/ablative sense aṭṭhā = 'for the purpose of'; whole = gavva-haraṇa-arthāya, 'in order to dispel pride'.
wordByWord.notes: States Indra's motive — a benevolent humbling, not a contest.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sakka < Sanskrit Śakra ('the mighty one'), epithet of Indra; nominative singular sakko.
wordByWord.notes: The Saudharma-Indra, chief of the gods, who attends the Jina.

wordByWord.etymology: deva ('god') + ahivaï (< Sanskrit adhipati, 'overlord'); appositional epithet of Śakra.
wordByWord.notes: Underlines that even the highest divine sovereign yields the true crown to the renunciant.

wordByWord.etymology: niya (< Sanskrit nija, 'one's own') + iḍḍhi (< Sanskrit ṛddhi, 'opulence, supernatural majesty'); accusative singular, object of daṁsae.
wordByWord.notes: The celestial wealth and miraculous forms with which Indra outshines the king's mortal pomp.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit daṁs- (causative) < Sanskrit darśayati ('causes to see, exhibits'); 3rd person singular.
wordByWord.notes: Narrative present, vivid 'and so Indra shows…'.
Forward-pointing: 'thus' — the manner is unfolded in the verses that follow (vv.50–55).

literalTranslation: On the mountain Daśārṇakūṭa, for the sake of removing the pride of (King) Daśārṇabhadra, Śakra, the lord of the gods, thus displays his own divine splendour.
idiomaticTranslation: Upon the Daśārṇa peak, to melt away the vanity of King Daśārṇabhadra, Śakra — sovereign of all the gods — unveils his own celestial magnificence in just this way.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse opens the Daśārṇakūṭa episode by naming the place, the proud king, and the divine agent of his transformation — leaving the spectacle itself to the verses that follow. King Daśārṇabhadra, hearing that Mahāvīra would arrive at dawn, had vowed to honour the Jina 'as no one in the three worlds has honoured him,' and set out with overwhelming royal pomp. The poet fixes the verse on the single word that drives the whole story: gavva, pride. Indra's response is framed not as rivalry but as compassion — gavva-haraṇaṭṭhā, 'for the sake of removing pride.' By stacking two sovereign titles on Indra (sakko, devāhivaï), the gāthā quietly sets up the lesson: even the king of gods, with splendour the mortal king cannot dream of matching, will himself bow lower than the renunciant. The narrative present daṁsae ('displays') and the cliff-hanger evaṁ ('thus') make this a curtain-raiser; the reader is told what Indra does and why, and invited onward to see how the king's pride collapses — and how he discovers that the one magnificence Indra cannot out-do is the act of renunciation, leading him to take dīkṣā on the spot.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse turns on māna (pride), one of the four kaṣāyas (passions: krodha, māna, māyā, lobha) that bind karma and obstruct liberation. Daśārṇabhadra's fault is subtle — his pride attaches to a meritorious act, devotion to the Jina — which is exactly why it must be exposed. Indra's display of ṛddhi teaches by contrast: worldly and even heavenly opulence is endless and surpassable, so it can never be the ground of true greatness; only the inward conquest of the self (the spirit of mokṣa-mārga) is unsurpassable. Thus a god's miraculous wealth becomes, paradoxically, a teaching aid pointing beyond all wealth.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: The reciter is warned that even religious devotion can breed ego — measuring one's worship against others'. The verse invites honest self-examination: am I bowing to the Jina, or performing for an audience? Like Daśārṇabhadra, one is asked to let pride be 'taken away' and to value inner renunciation over outward show.

crossReferences.text: Triṣaṣṭi-śalākā-puruṣa-caritra, Book X (life of Mahāvīra), Daśārṇabhadra episode
crossReferences.source: Ācārya Hemacandra; Eng. tr. Helen M. Johnson, Gaekwad's Oriental Series

crossReferences.text: Daśārṇabhadra-caritra (narrative tradition on the king's pride, Indra's humbling, and his renunciation)

sources.title: Triṣaṣṭi-śalākā-puruṣa-caritra — Daśārṇabhadra (Part 1)
sources.consultedFor: Hemacandra's canonical account of Daśārṇabhadra's vow, his pomp, and Indra arriving to humble his pride at the samavasaraṇa

sources.title: Riddhi (Ṛddhi) — definitions across traditions incl. Jainism/Prakrit
sources.consultedFor: Glossing iḍḍhi < ṛddhi as 'opulence, supernatural majesty/splendour'

sources.title: Dasharnabhadra Charitram — Book Summary
sources.consultedFor: Confirming the legend's arc: pride, Indra's grander procession, the king's humbling and renunciation (dīkṣā)

sources.title: King Dasharnabhadra's Inspirational Story
sources.consultedFor: Corroborating Daśārṇapura/Daśārṇagiri setting, the king's desire to surpass all worshippers, and the moral of humbled pride

notes: The verse text is clear and the glosses secure; gavva (garva), iḍḍhi (ṛddhi) and daṁsae (darśayati causative) are standard Prakrit→Sanskrit correspondences. 'દસન્ન ભદ્દસ્સ' is printed with a word-space but is the single royal name Daśārṇabhadra in the genitive; tokenized as written per fidelity rules. haraṇaṭṭhā = haraṇa + aṭṭhā (artha), a purpose-sense compound; 'gavva-haraṇaṭṭhā' read as 'for removing pride.' 'Daśārṇakūṭa' (= Daśārṇagiri, near Daśārṇapura) is the hill of the episode; some editions/retellings vary slightly in place-name spelling. evaṁ ('thus') is proleptic, pointing to the description continued in the following verses; this gāthā is the episode's opening frame, so its 'display' is announced here and elaborated onward.
